text: The surgeon dissects along the obliterated umbilical ligament, pulling it medially to define the vesico-hypogastric fascia and the paravesical space. The assistant aids by pulling the ligament medially, while the surgeon continues dissection in the loose areolar tissue, revealing the obturator nerve.

The surgeon dissects nodal tissue over the external iliac vessels, proceeding from lateral to medial. Tissue is cleared over the femoral canal and dissected off the medial border of the external iliac vein, pulling it medially. The dissection continues proximally towards the bifurcation of the iliac vessels, clearing nodal tissue and identifying the proximal part of the obturator nerve.

The surgeon identifies and carefully dissects the proximal part of the obturator nerve using a cold swipe technique, clearing nodal tissue around it. The obturator vessels are identified, dissected, clipped, and divided using monopolar scissors. A vessel feeding the nodal tissue is also dissected, clipped, and divided. The surgeon continues to clear nodal tissue over the internal iliac artery and the obturator nerve in the distal segment.

The surgeon dissects and clears all nodal tissue from the obturator nerve, highlighting the difference in dissection depth required for rectal adenocarcinoma compared to gynecological malignancies, as the procedure continues towards the pelvic floor.

The surgeon clears tissue over the obliterated umbilical ligament, dissecting down along its lateral aspect. The dissection continues along the branches of the internal iliac artery, progressing towards the inferior vesicle artery.

The surgeon clears tissue along the lateral aspect of the bladder, proceeding towards the pelvic floor. The inferior vesicle artery is encountered, and nodal tissue over the artery is meticulously cleared. The dissection then moves laterally over the pudendal vessels, which are clipped and divided after clearing all surrounding tissue.

The surgeon clears all nodal tissue over the pelvic floor, completing the pelvic lymph node dissection. The specimen is delivered in total after the nodal tissue is reflected and dissected off. The final field of dissection is inspected, revealing intact urethra hypogastric fascia and preserved hypogastric nerve on the medial aspect. Dissection along the branches of the internal iliac vessels shows the internal iliac vein crossing anterior to the branches of the internal iliac artery, a noted anatomical variation. All nodal tissue along the inferior vesicle artery and pelvic floor is cleared, with critical structures identified and preserved.
